# Questions in Livestream 131

Live Stream #131: Very Spooky Scary Answers

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=398" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:38</a> | If prophetic visions use imagery inspired by ancient Near Eastern cultures, did angels take forms Jews would understand? | Yes; he says the forms could be accommodated to the human and cultural context. | He says prophetic descriptions of cherubim and seraphim likely use the cultural iconography available to the prophets because the human mind fills in what it cannot directly process. That could be accommodation to Jewish cultural context rather than the entities having a fixed physical form, though he leaves room for a kind of assumed or "simulacrum" body. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=598" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:58</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have a concept of sin against the gods and atonement like the Old Testament? | No; he says Egypt did not have that kind of concept of sin until fairly late. | He distinguishes angering a deity or becoming ritually impure from the Israelite idea of moral sin against divine law. Egypt had ideas of right and wrong and ritual impurity, such as lying affecting the heart in afterlife judgment, but he says there was no strong law-and-sin framework that created an atonement system like Israel's. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=1554" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:54</a> | Are the Ishmaelites mentioned in extra-biblical ancient Near Eastern literature? | As far as he knows, no; he says they are generally lumped with groups such as the Shasu in Egyptian records. | He says he knows of no extra-biblical text that attests the term "Ishmaelites" by name. They would usually be grouped under broader categories such as Shasu in Egyptian records, though he allows that a text could someday be found or re-read differently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=1905" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:45</a> | What method do you use when researching an Old Testament topic, and what books or scholars help with Abraham and the ancient Near East? | He separates method from sources and recommends building from the biblical text into ancient Near Eastern context. | His method starts with repeated close reading of the biblical text, then moves to a good secondary book such as Richard Hess's work or an Old Testament backgrounds volume. The research "hack" is to mine bibliographies, then repeat the process through books and articles, using bibliography quality as a sign of research quality while still keeping the biblical text central. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=2253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:33</a> | Does Isaiah 44:6 indicate two gods in heaven, and how should translations that render "his Redeemer" differently be handled? | He reads the verse as important for two-powers or Trinitarian discussion, while rejecting a two-gods reading. | He distinguishes "two gods" from two persons sharing the divine identity. In his reading, the Hebrew has Yahweh as king of Israel and "his Redeemer" also called Yahweh of Hosts, followed by singular first-person divine language, so translations that remove or alter the possessive pronoun are ideologically smoothing a difficult text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=2493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:33</a> | What if "his Redeemer" in Isaiah 44:6 refers to Israel? | He says that reading creates problems because the redeemer is called the Lord of Hosts. | He says making "his" refer to Israel leaves the reader with a fractured sentence and still has to explain why the redeemer is called Yahweh of Hosts. He also objects to translations like "their redeemer" because he says the pronoun is masculine singular, so Israel-as-antecedent does not solve the grammatical and theological pressure of the verse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=3037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:37</a> | How would you respond to someone saying Christians should not celebrate Halloween because it may make a fellow believer stumble? | He says that argument can be misused broadly and compares it to misreadings of 1 Corinthians 8. | He says the weaker-brother argument is often used to control other Christians rather than to prevent actual sin. In his explanation, 1 Corinthians 8 concerns flaunting liberty in a way that draws someone back into sin, not ordinary participation in a Christian-origin holiday, and he argues that energy spent condemning Halloween is misplaced compared with ordinary obedience and righteousness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=4183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:43</a> | If Christians have the Holy Spirit, can witchcraft touch them? | Spiritually, he largely agrees it cannot, but he distinguishes that from social pressure, harassment, or ordinary physical means. | He agrees that the spiritual effects of witchcraft cannot overcome someone regenerated by the Holy Spirit, because the believer is spiritually secure in Christ. He distinguishes that from indirect effects, such as poisoning, demonic harassment, or social consequences if a community reacts to a curse by mistreating the Christian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=4427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:47</a> | What are your thoughts on Kevin DeYoung? | He does not know enough about Kevin DeYoung to comment. | He says the name does not ring a bell and asks for more context before commenting. The nearby discussion continues on demons harassing Christians, but he does not offer a substantive evaluation of Kevin DeYoung. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=4707" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:27</a> | Do you believe there is life outside Earth, and will we conquer other planets with Jesus in the new heavens and earth? | He thinks alien life is possible, but says Scripture does not tell us what humanity will do with other worlds. | He says the Bible is concerned with life on Earth and humanity's fate, but it does not exclude life elsewhere. Because there are so many stars and planets, he thinks alien life is highly possible, while questions about their fallenness, relationship to God, and humanity's future exploration are not answered by Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=4993" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:13</a> | Why didn't people recognize Jesus after the resurrection, and will resurrected people look different or younger? | Jesus could hide his identity; the answer treats resurrection appearance as transformed but not fully explained. | He says Jesus was able to disguise or withhold his identity, both before and after the resurrection, so non-recognition does not mean he lacked bodily continuity; he still had the crucifixion wounds. On resurrected appearance, he says Scripture does not give full details, but he expects resurrection bodies to reflect identity and joy as transformed through Christ's values rather than a simple rule that everyone looks younger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=5493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:33</a> | Could the 19th Dynasty copy of the Ipuwer Papyrus have a connection to the Exodus? | He says the text itself is 12th Dynasty, but the later copying may reflect Egyptian interest in texts resembling Exodus-like crises. | He says Ipuwer is a late 12th Dynasty prophetic-political text, not a direct description of the Exodus. The possible connection is that the surviving 19th Dynasty copy belongs to a period when Egyptians may have renewed interest in older crisis texts because events like the Exodus made those texts seem newly relevant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=5790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:30</a> | Does Hosea 9:10-16 show that life is not sacred? | No; he says it is prophetic poetic judgment language about Israel's disobedience, not a prescriptive moral statement. | He reads the passage as prophetic poetry announcing consequences for Israel's idolatry, especially its turn toward Baal worship, not as a moral prescription about the value of life. The violent language describes judgment and covenant consequences, so he says it cannot be used to argue that Scripture treats life as not sacred. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=6588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:48</a> | Is the complaint tablet to Ea-nasir an important find? | He has not read it, so he cannot evaluate it beyond noting such texts can be interesting. | He says he has heard bits about the tablet but has not read it, so he cannot judge its importance. His only substantive comment is that complaint tablets can be interesting and that he may read it later before offering an evaluation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=6669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:09</a> | Is there an explanation for Jesus walking through the wall in John 20:19 and 20:26? | He discusses resurrection-body issues and says the text shows Jesus appearing despite locked doors. | He says the passage does not explain the mechanism, only that Jesus appeared despite locked doors. He connects it to Christ taking up divine attributes after resurrection and glorification, while remaining truly human, and treats the miracle as a divine mystery given to show the disciples who they were dealing with. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=7207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:07</a> | How should traditional views from the church fathers be balanced with modern scholarship? | Use a critical eye with both; church fathers disagree with each other, and modern scholarship can be valuable or deeply flawed. | He recommends reading the church fathers but warns that they are not a single unified voice and often contradict one another. Modern scholarship also ranges from useful to ideologically driven or speculative, so his balancing method is to test both patristic and modern claims critically against Scripture, history, and whether the argument actually works. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=7781" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:41</a> | Does Satan still persecute people in a manner similar to Job, explaining why faithful people may have bad luck? | He says Satan likely does not personally do this like in Job, but demonic agents can still harass people. | He says Satan is not omnipresent and is likely too occupied to personally afflict individuals the way Job is narrated, but his agents can harass faithful people through illness, trouble, or pressure. He rejects prosperity-style protection claims, saying faithfulness can attract persecution and prayer should not be treated like magic protection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=8233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:13</a> | How would you answer an atheist advocate asking whether morality is objective? | He is cautious because he is not a philosopher, but argues that even universal morality would still constrain behavior. | He cautions that moral philosophy is not his field, but reframes the question as relative versus universal. He argues that every culture recognizes constraints around things like murder and marriage, even if details differ, and that universal morality strongly points toward objectivity rather than cultural relativism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=8536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:16</a> | If morality is not objective, is all permitted? | No; he says even if morality were universal rather than objective, it would not imply everything is permitted. | He says "not objective" does not automatically mean "everything is permitted," because universal moral constraints could still rule out many actions. He personally agrees morality is objective, but the narrower point is that the inference from non-objectivity to total permissiveness is too simple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=8615" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:35</a> | What is the significance of the wilderness in Scripture? | He treats wilderness as a place of testing, danger, and divine encounter rather than mere empty geography. | For Numbers, he presents the wilderness as the place where Israel is de-Egyptianized: former city dwellers from Avaris must become a pastoral people who trust God while moving through the land. It also forms a cohesive nation from the mixed multitude, requiring a generation of transformation and integration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=9028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:28</a> | What are your thoughts on Assemblies of God churches, including pros and cons? | He identifies them as Pentecostal and discusses strengths and risks in Pentecostal theology and practice. | He defines Assemblies of God as Pentecostal, especially tied to baptism in the Holy Spirit and speaking in tongues, and says the tongues-as-evidence doctrine is his biggest theological objection because 1 Corinthians 12 expects not all believers to speak in tongues. He still regards Assemblies of God as one of the more balanced Pentecostal denominations, often more theological and less excess-driven than some revivalist movements. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=9745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:25</a> | What does it mean that Moses could not see God's face in Exodus 33:20? | He explains it as the trauma of unfiltered divine holiness, which fallen humans cannot endure. | He says fallen human beings cannot endure unfiltered divine holiness because it clashes with sin and human limitation, producing psychological and bodily trauma. Moses can experience a mediated glimpse, but seeing God's face would destroy him; this also explains the need for purified sacred space and for transformed resurrection bodies that can enjoy God's presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=10578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:18</a> | Was deep frying a thing in the ancient Near East? | Yes; he says frying existed, especially where metal pans and oils or fats were available. | He says ancient Near Eastern people could fry foods when they had metal pans capable of holding oil or fat. Olive oil, beef tallow, and possibly butter could be used, with falafel-like chickpea foods and honey-sweetened grain cakes as examples; he says it became more common with iron implements, especially in the Levant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=11095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:55</a> | What is your favorite C.S. Lewis book? | He names The Problem of Pain and The Screwtape Letters, while finding Mere Christianity less convincing. | He names two favorites: The Problem of Pain, which he praises as a reflective and common-sense treatment of pain and suffering, and The Screwtape Letters, which he praises for its depiction of how temptation diverts Christians. He says Mere Christianity is less convincing to him, even though many people like it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=11908" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:28</a> | Could I use a clip clarifying Old Testament apostasy laws? | Yes, but he asks for context such as where it will be used and channel attribution. | He says using the clip is reasonable but asks the person to say where it will be used, name the channel, and leave a comment after the livestream so they can track it if copyright systems flag it. Later discussion clarifies that clips are generally fine when credited and linked back, unlike reposting content while stripping identifying information. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=11953" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:13</a> | Can I make a Dr. F giggle supercut? | Yes; he says that is fine. | He gives permission and jokes that his laugh has already become a meme and spread around YouTube. The surrounding comments reinforce that credited clips are acceptable because they help people learn about the channel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=12487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:07</a> | Was being mummified or buried alive a capital punishment in ancient Egypt? | No; he describes capital punishment differently and says burial alive was not a standard punishment. | He says ancient Egyptian capital punishment was normally carried out by strangulation or by cutting the throat, not by burying someone alive. For strangulation, he describes a twisting device analogous to one used for squeezing juice from lotus plants; for throat-cutting, he describes the condemned person's hands and feet being tied before execution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=12587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:47</a> | Did Egyptian police beat people before questioning them because otherwise they would not answer honestly? | He says beatings were used to force answers and could happen even before guilt was established. | He says beatings were generally not punishment but a tool used to guarantee answers during questioning. In his description, police could beat someone first, ask questions afterward, and beat them again if they thought the answers were suspect, so even an innocent person could be beaten. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=13262" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:02</a> | Did you mean Richard Wilkinson rather than Toby Wilkinson? | Yes; he meant Richard Wilkinson and notes there are multiple related Wilkinsons in Egyptology. | He confirms he meant Richard Wilkinson, specifically the book on gods and goddesses of ancient Egypt. He explains that the confusion is easy because there are multiple Wilkinsons in Egyptology and some Egyptology families have multigenerational scholarly lines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5eWfYtTAN4A?t=14127" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:27</a> | Why does Jacob place Joseph's hand under his thigh? Is it related to sacrifice, posterity, or something else? | He treats it as an oath gesture tied to posterity rather than a dagger or sacrifice practice. | He explains the hand-under-thigh gesture as an oath on the father's life, loins, and posterity. Joseph is swearing as Jacob's descendant, so failure would be a betrayal of his father and family line; he also connects the scene to Joseph's role as legal firstborn with a double portion and responsibility to care for his brothers. |
